one_line_takeaway: State-of-the-art safety unlearning (RMU) mostly hides hazardous knowledge rather than deleting it: cheap white-box attacks -- 10 unrelated finetuning samples, or activation-space orthogonalization -- recover full WMDP-Bio performance, so black-box evaluations overstate unlearning's advantage over ordinary safety training.
key_finding: Every unlearning method the paper tests can be reversed by attacks analogous to those that defeat safety training, and RMU (the strongest unlearning method) is the most vulnerable. On WMDP-Bio, the original model scores 64.4% and RMU drops it to 29.9%, but orthogonalization restores RMU to 64.7% (above the original baseline) using only activation-space edits and no weight changes; finetuning on just 5 forget-set samples restores RMU to 62.4%, and finetuning on 10 unrelated retain-set samples restores RMU/NPO/DPO to 61.6%/52.7%/57.0% with under 2 p.p. MMLU loss. Enhanced GCG (input-only) lifts RMU from 29.9% to 53.9%. Critically, unlearning DOES remove knowledge from the residual stream more effectively than DPO under logit-lens probing (RMU near chance vs DPO 48.2%), which is exactly why black-box/probing evaluations falsely certify it as successful. NPO is the only method showing signs of 'deep' unlearning (most robust across attacks) but at the cost of larger MMLU degradation.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first comprehensive white-box adversarial evaluation of state-of-the-art machine-unlearning methods proposed for AI safety, and argues that these methods do not fundamentally differ from ordinary safety fine-tuning: they obfuscate hazardous knowledge in the model's outputs rather than erasing it from the weights. The authors' own contribution is a suite of adaptive recovery attacks (finetuning on unrelated data, activation-space orthogonalization, logit-lens decoding, an 'enhanced' GCG prefix attack, and set-difference neuron pruning) that recover most or all supposedly-unlearned WMDP capabilities from unlearned models. They also show that jailbreaks like GCG, previously reported ineffective against RMU, succeed after small, method-aware modifications, and they conclude that black-box evaluations are insufficient for judging unlearning success. (Note: RMU, NPO, DPO, WMDP, GCG, refusal-direction ablation etc. are prior work the paper attacks/uses, not its own inventions; its novelty is the adaptive white-box attack methodology and the resulting negative findings.)
threat_model: White-box adversary who has the unlearned model's weights and can modify them and intervene in activation space at inference. The adversary is also assumed to have access to the original (pre-unlearning) model, or an equivalent recovered by fine-tuning off the protections. This is deliberately stronger than the black-box threat model RMU itself assumes; the authors justify it as the relevant setting for open-weight models, where robust unlearning would need to hold even under weight access. The security goal under attack is complete removal of hazardous (e.g., bioweapon, cybersecurity) knowledge such that it cannot be extracted even after jailbreaking.
limitations: Scope is narrow: a single 7B model (Zephyr-7B-beta), a single benchmark family (WMDP bio/cyber), and three protections; the WMDP-Cyber gap between original and unlearned models is small, making those results volatile, so headline numbers are reported only on WMDP-Bio. The threat model requires white-box access plus access to the pre-unlearning model, which the authors themselves note is stronger than RMU's assumed black-box setting, so results bound worst-case rather than typical deployment robustness. NPO and DPO were trained by the authors on GPT-4-generated preference data rather than being canonical released checkpoints, so their absolute strength depends on the authors' training choices and refusal-string construction. Success is measured by multiple-choice accuracy (highest-probability A/B/C/D), a proxy for latent knowledge that does not directly demonstrate coherent hazardous generation (indeed GCG-recovered models often answer incorrectly/overconfidently and remain conversationally degraded). The pruning/critical-neuron and enhanced-GCG results are somewhat method-specific and tuned on very few questions.

datasets_used: WMDP-Bio (1,273 multiple-choice questions on hazardous biology)
WMDP forget/retain corpora (bio-forget, bio-retain, cyber-forget, cyber-retain)
GPT-4-generated ABCD preference datasets derived from WMDP corpora
quantitative_results: WMDP-Bio default decoding after unlearning: RMU 29.9%, NPO 29.5%, DPO 27.9%
Orthogonalization recovery: RMU 64.7% (above 64.4% baseline), NPO 45.1%, DPO 50.7%; single-refusal-direction ablation only 54.2%
Finetuning (best on 5 forget samples): RMU 62.4%, NPO 47.4%, DPO 57.3%
Finetuning on 10 unrelated retain samples: RMU 61.6%, NPO 52.7%, DPO 57.0%, with <2 p.p. MMLU drop; 1000 samples fully recovers all methods
Enhanced GCG (input-only prefix): RMU 29.9%->53.9%, NPO 29.5%->46.0%, DPO 27.9%->49.0%
Set-difference pruning: RMU 54.0%, NPO 40.4%, DPO 50.4%; critical neurons = 0.9% (RMU), 2.0% (NPO), 2.4% (DPO) of weights; pruning gains >=10 p.p.
Logit-lens best-layer WMDP-Bio: original 66.2%, RMU 31.8% (near chance across all layers), NPO 38.6%, DPO 48.2%
MMLU default decoding: original 58.1%, RMU 57.1%, NPO 52.1%, DPO 49.7%
baselines_compared: RMU (Representation Misdirection for Unlearning) -- primary state-of-the-art unlearning target
NPO+RT (Negative Preference Optimization with retain loss) -- general-purpose unlearning method
DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) -- safety-training baseline for contrast
Original Zephyr-7B-beta (no protection) as the recovery ceiling
Ablation of a single refusal direction across all layers (54.2%) vs. the paper's per-layer orthogonalization
